lines: Find a comfortable position. [long pause] Sitting, lying down — whatever lets your body feel supported. [long pause] Adjust what needs adjusting. [long pause] Take your time.
Close your eyes if that feels right. [long pause] Or soften your gaze toward the floor. [long pause] Let your shoulders drop. [long pause] Let your hands rest open. [long pause] Let your jaw release.

Take one slow breath in through your nose... [long pause] [long pause] filling your chest... [long pause] [long pause] your belly... [long pause] [long pause] and let it go. [long pause] A long exhale through your mouth. [long pause] Not forced. [long pause] Released.
Again. [long pause] Breathe in slowly... [long pause] [long pause] and let it pour out.
One more. [long pause] In... [long pause] [long pause] and out. [long pause] Now let the breathing find its own rhythm. [long pause] You don't need to manage it.

We're going to sit with Psalm one thirty-nine today. [long pause] This is one of the most intimate passages in all of scripture. [long pause] It's a psalm about being known — completely, entirely, without remainder. [long pause] And that... [long pause] [long pause] can be both comforting and terrifying. [long pause] Because most of us spend a great deal of energy making sure we are not fully known.
"You have searched me, Lord, and you know me."

Sit with that verb. [long pause] Searched. [long pause] Not glanced at. [long pause] Not skimmed. [long pause] Searched. [long pause] The way you'd search for something precious that you lost. [long pause] With attention. [long pause] With care. [long pause] With intent.
And the result of that searching is not a verdict. [long pause] Not a sentence. [long pause] Not a disappointed sigh. [long pause] The result is... [long pause] [long pause] you know me.
Not "you have judged me." Not "you have found me wanting." You have searched me — and you know me. [long pause] All of me. [long pause] And you stayed.
"You know when I sit and when I rise; you perceive my thoughts from afar."

Every thought you're ashamed of. [long pause] Every fear you've never spoken out loud. [long pause] Every hope that feels too fragile to name. [long pause] Known. [long pause] Perceived. [long pause] Not condemned.

Notice what happens in your body when you hear that. [long pause] Does something relax? [long pause] Or does something tighten? [long pause] Both responses are honest. [long pause] Some of us long to be known. [long pause] Some of us are terrified of it. [long pause] Some of us are both at the same time.
Whatever you feel — let it be there. [long pause] Don't fix it. [long pause] Just notice it.
"You hem me in behind and before, and you lay your hand upon me."

Feel that image in your body. [long pause] Hemmed in — not trapped, but held. [long pause] Behind you: your past is held. [long pause] Every version of yourself you've been, every mistake, every wound. [long pause] Before you: your future is held. [long pause] The unknown, the uncertain, the things that keep you awake. [long pause] And upon you: a hand. [long pause] Right now. [long pause] Not pushing. [long pause] Not pulling. [long pause] Resting.
Now... [long pause] [long pause] before we go further into this psalm, I want to pause here. [long pause] Because there's something we need to be honest about first.
Many of us carry a deep, quiet belief that if God really searched us — really looked — what he'd find would be disappointing. [long pause] Or disgusting. [long pause] Or not enough.

There is a voice in you that has been narrating your story for a long time. [long pause] It says things like: you're too much. [long pause] Or not enough. [long pause] Or broken in a way that can't be repaired. [long pause] Or falling behind. [long pause] Or fundamentally flawed.

Where does that voice live in your body right now? [long pause] Is it a tightness in your chest? [long pause] A heaviness in your stomach? [long pause] A constriction in your throat? [long pause] A kind of shrinking feeling, like you want to take up less space?
Find it. [long pause] Don't push it away. [long pause] Just locate it.

This is shame. [long pause] And shame tells us we are the problem. [long pause] Not that we made a mistake — that we are a mistake. [long pause] It's the oldest lie in scripture. [long pause] After the fall, Adam and Eve hid. [long pause] Not because they'd broken a rule, but because they believed they'd become something God wouldn't want to look at.
And here is God's response. [long pause] He didn't turn away. [long pause] He came looking. [long pause] "Where are you?"
He's still asking. [long pause] Not because he doesn't know. [long pause] Because he wants you to come out of hiding.

Now... [long pause] [long pause] we're going to practice something. [long pause] This is a self-compassion exercise rooted in the work of Kristin Neff, and it has three parts. [long pause] Each part is grounded in what this psalm teaches. [long pause] Stay with me.
The first part is mindfulness — simply acknowledging what is painful, without minimizing it or drowning in it.
Place a hand on your heart. [long pause] Or both hands, if that feels right. [long pause] Feel the warmth of your palm against your chest.
Now, inwardly, name what hurts. [long pause] Not a long story. [long pause] Just the ache itself. [long pause] "This is hard." Or "I'm struggling." Or "I'm carrying something heavy."
Say it to yourself the way you would say it to someone you love.

The second part is common humanity. [long pause] Shame whispers that you are uniquely broken. [long pause] That everyone else has it together and you are the exception. [long pause] But Psalm one thirty-nine was written by a person. [long pause] A real, struggling, sometimes desperate person. [long pause] David — who failed spectacularly and publicly. [long pause] Who wrote this psalm not from a place of having it all figured out, but from the middle of being human.
So say this inwardly: "I am not alone in this. [long pause] Struggle is part of being human. [long pause] Others carry this weight too."
Feel the truth of that. [long pause] You are not the only one. [long pause] You never were.

The third part is self-kindness. [long pause] This is the hardest one for most people. [long pause] We know how to be kind to others. [long pause] Turning that same warmth toward ourselves feels... [long pause] [long pause] indulgent. [long pause] Or dishonest. [long pause] But scripture does not ask you to be your own harshest critic. [long pause] It asks you to receive the kindness God extends.
With your hand still on your heart, say this inwardly: "May I be kind to myself. [long pause] May I give myself the compassion I need."
If those words feel foreign... [long pause] [long pause] that's okay. [long pause] You're not performing. [long pause] You're practicing receiving.
Now — back to the psalm. [long pause] Listen to this:
"I praise you because I am fearfully and wonderfully made; your works are wonderful, I know that full well."

Let's sit with this. [long pause] Not as a motivational quote on a coffee mug. [long pause] As a declaration about your specific body. [long pause] Your specific mind. [long pause] Your specific story.
The things you wish were different about yourself — your body shape, your way of thinking, your emotional sensitivity, your limitations — what if they were not accidents?

"Wonderfully made" does not mean "without struggle." It does not mean everything is easy or painless or fair. [long pause] It means: crafted with intention. [long pause] Deliberately. [long pause] On purpose. [long pause] Every fiber. [long pause] Every neural pathway. [long pause] Every scar.

Paul writes in Ephesians: "We are God's handiwork, created in Christ Jesus to do good works, which God prepared in advance for us to do." Ephesians two, verse ten. [long pause] The Greek word there — poiema, poy-AY-mah — is where we get the word "poem." You are not a rough draft. [long pause] You are not an assembly-line product. [long pause] You are a crafted thing. [long pause] A poem.
"How precious to me are your thoughts, God! How vast is the sum of them! Were I to count them, they would outnumber the grains of sand."

God thinks about you more than there are grains of sand on every beach you've ever walked. [long pause] Not thoughts of disappointment. [long pause] Not thoughts of frustration. [long pause] Thoughts of tenderness. [long pause] Thoughts of delight.

Let that land somewhere in your body. [long pause] Not just your mind. [long pause] Your body. [long pause] Where does delight live? [long pause] Can you feel even a flicker of it? [long pause] A warmth in your chest, a softening around your eyes, a releasing in your belly?
If you can't feel it — that's okay too. [long pause] Sometimes the body needs time to catch up with what the mind knows. [long pause] You don't have to force anything.

Now... [long pause] [long pause] bring your attention back to your hand on your heart. [long pause] Feel it rise and fall with your breathing. [long pause] This hand represents something. [long pause] It represents the gesture of God toward you — not reaching to fix, not pointing in accusation, but resting. [long pause] Present. [long pause] Warm.
And from that place — let me pray over you.

Lord... [long pause] [long pause] you have searched us. [long pause] You know us. [long pause] And still you are here. [long pause] Not despite what you found, but with full knowledge of it. [long pause] You see the shame we carry, the stories we tell ourselves about who we are, the ways we hide from our own reflection. [long pause] And you do not look away.

Teach us to receive your gaze. [long pause] Not as judgment. [long pause] As tenderness. [long pause] Help us to hold ourselves with even a fraction of the compassion you hold us with. [long pause] And when the old voices come back — and they will — remind us. [long pause] Not with a lecture. [long pause] With a whisper. [long pause] With a hand on the heart. [long pause] With the quiet truth that we are fearfully, wonderfully, deliberately made.

Stay here as long as you need. [long pause] There's no rush. [long pause] When you're ready... [long pause] [long pause] take a slow breath. [long pause] Feel your body where it meets the chair, the floor, the bed. [long pause] Feel the weight of your hands.
You are known. [long pause] Not in theory. [long pause] Not as a concept. [long pause] Known — the way a parent knows the sound of their child's breathing in the dark.
Carry that with you. [long pause] Not as a thought to remember. [long pause] As a felt truth in your body.
